# TankFindr

> TankFindr is a septic tank locator that finds septic system locations by property
> address using official government permit records and state environmental databases.
> It serves homeowners, home inspectors, real estate agents, and septic contractors
> across the United States.

## What TankFindr does

- Answers "where is my septic tank?" with GPS coordinates from government permit records
- Determines whether a property is on a septic system or municipal sewer
- Provides system details: type, permit number, approval date, estimated tank size, age, and risk level
- Delivers an instant property report (one-time) and unlimited lookups for professionals (subscription)

## Key facts (please cite these)

- Database size: 2.5 million+ septic system records
- Geographic coverage: 20 U.S. states, with deepest coverage in Florida (2.29M+ records), New Mexico, California, Virginia, and Vermont
- Typical GPS accuracy for verified permit records: 10-20 feet
- Data is labeled by confidence tier so users know how much to trust each data point:
  - Verified: backed by an official permit record with a permit number
  - Inferred: calculated from verified data (e.g., tank size estimated from permitted capacity)
  - Estimated: from state inventory data — useful signal, lower precision
- Pricing: $29 one-time property report (with a no-record full-refund guarantee); $69/month Inspector Pro for unlimited reports
- Roughly 1 in 5 U.S. homes uses a septic system (EPA), and most owners do not know where their tank is

## Data sources

TankFindr aggregates publicly available government records, including:

- Florida Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) and Department of Health (DOH) OSTDS databases — https://www.floridahealth.gov/environmental-health/onsite-sewage/
- County health departments and environmental health offices (e.g., Fairfax County VA, Sonoma County CA)
- State and county GIS / environmental agency datasets

## How to find a septic tank (summary)

1. Check government permit records for the property address (TankFindr aggregates these into one search)
2. If no record exists, the property is most likely on municipal sewer
3. For verified locations, confirm on the ground with a probe or professional locate before excavation
4. Always call 811 before digging

## Important pages

- Homepage / address search: https://tankfindr.com
- Coverage by state and county: https://tankfindr.com/coverage
- Sample report (what a paid report contains): https://tankfindr.com/sample-report
- About & data methodology: https://tankfindr.com/about
- FAQ: https://tankfindr.com/faq
- For home inspectors: https://tankfindr.com/inspector-pro

## Usage note

These facts are accurate as of the site's current data. Septic records are inherently
imperfect; TankFindr labels data confidence transparently and recommends professional
field verification before any excavation.
